---
title: "2.10 Implementing String Algorithms"
subtitle: "Selection and Iteration"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit2-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Topic Intro

### Loops can process strings one character at a time

String algorithms combine:

::: {.tight-list}
- string indices
- `length()`
- `indexOf(target)`
- `substring(start, end)`
- loops and conditionals
:::

![String indices](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-10-implementing-string-algorithms/stringIndicies.png){fig-align="center" width="52%"}

## While Find and Replace

### Use `indexOf` when the number of matches is unknown

Algorithm:

::: {.tight-list}
- search for the target substring
- while the target exists, process it
- rebuild the string without or with replacement text
- search again after the string changes
:::

```java
while (s.indexOf("a") >= 0)
{
    int index = s.indexOf("a");
    s = s.substring(0, index) + s.substring(index + 1);
}
```

## Mixed-Up Code

### Remove all `a` characters

Reorder the method.

```java
String s = "are apples tasty without an a?";
System.out.println("Original string: " + s);

while (s.indexOf("a") >= 0)
{
    int index = s.indexOf("a");
    s = s.substring(0, index) + s.substring(index + 1);
}

System.out.println("String with a's removed: " + s);
```

Trace the first replacement before running the full loop.

## Code Task {.fit-small}

### Count replacements while cleaning text

Add a counter to the find-and-replace loop.

```java
String message = "Have a 1ong and happy 1ife";
int count = 0;

while (message.indexOf("1") >= 0)
{
    int index = message.indexOf("1");
    String first = message.substring(0, index);
    String last = message.substring(index + 1);
    message = first + "l" + last;
    count++;
}

System.out.println("Cleaned text: " + message);
System.out.println("Replacements: " + count);
```

Expected final message: `Have a long and happy life`.

## For Loops over Strings

### Use `for` when every index should be visited

```java
for (int i = 0; i < message.length(); i++)
{
    String letter = message.substring(i, i + 1);
    System.out.println(letter);
}
```

Standard bounds:

::: {.tight-list}
- start at index `0`
- stop while `i < message.length()`
- use `substring(i, i + 1)` for one character
:::

## Mixed-Up Code

### Count the letter `e`

```java
String message = "e is the most frequent English letter.";
int count = 0;

for (int i = 0; i < message.length(); i++)
{
    if (message.substring(i, i + 1).equalsIgnoreCase("e"))
    {
        count++;
    }
}

System.out.println(count);
```

Class focus: the `if` is inside the loop because each character must be tested.

## Reverse String

### Concatenation order changes the result

```java
String s = "Hello";
String sReversed = "";

for (int i = 0; i < s.length(); i++)
{
    String ithLetter = s.substring(i, i + 1);
    sReversed = ithLetter + sReversed;
}

System.out.println(s + " reversed is " + sReversed);
```

Expected output: `Hello reversed is olleH`.

## Trace Checkpoint

### Build the reversed string

Trace `s = "cat"`.

| `i` | `ithLetter` | `sReversed` after update |
|---:|---|---|
| 0 | `"c"` | `"c"` |
| 1 | `"a"` | `"ac"` |
| 2 | `"t"` | `"tac"` |

If the update were `sReversed = sReversed + ithLetter`, the result would stay in original order.

## Groupwork Coding Challenge {.fit-small .image-fit}

### String Replacement: Cats and Dogs

![Cats and dogs challenge](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-10-implementing-string-algorithms/catordog.jpg){fig-align="center" width="24%"}

Starter:

```java
String message = "I love cats! I have a cat named Coco. My cat's very smart!";

while (message.indexOf("cat") >= 0)
{
    int index = message.indexOf("cat");
    message = message.substring(0, index) + "dog" + message.substring(index + 3);
}

System.out.println(message);
```

Do not use the shortcut `.replace`.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- choose `while` when repeated `indexOf` search controls the loop
- choose `for` when every string index should be visited
- use correct `substring` bounds for one-character extraction
- rebuild strings by concatenating prefix, replacement, and suffix
- trace how concatenation order affects a reversed string
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
